# Introduction to the Past Passive Participle

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

When you dive into the world of Classical Sanskrit, you’ll quickly notice a grammatical form that pops up everywhere, more frequently in later texts. This form is called the “past passive participle” (which I hereafter abbreviate unofficially as PPP), and it’s so common that you can’t ignore it for long. If you’re not a grammar enthusiast, don’t worry! Think of the PPP as a versatile tool that adds colour and depth to sentences, much like spices in Indian cooking. It’s not merely a grammatical rule ; it’s a way to vividly describe actions and states in a compact, elegant form. Let’s break it down step by step, with plenty of examples, so you can see why this form is both useful and fascinating.

Imagine you’re reading a story in English, and you come across phrases like “*the **fallen** leaf*”, “*the **risen** sun*”, “*the **defeated** warrior*”, or “*the **sunken** treasure*”. These phrases paint a picture, don’t they? The words in bold—“*fallen*,” “*risen*,” “*defeated*,” “*sunken*”—are doing something special. They describe a noun (like “*leaf*” or “*warrior*”) by telling us about an action that happened to it in the past, and that action was done to the noun (the noun is the suffering object of the verb), not by the noun (the noun is not the doer subject of the verb). For example, the “defeated” warrior didn’t defeat someone else; the warrior was defeated. That’s what makes these words passive. And since the action (defeating, falling, sinking) happened before the moment we’re talking about, they’re also past. This combination — past and passive — gives us the name past passive participle.

In Sanskrit, the PPP works in a similar way. It’s a word derived from a verb but behaves like an adjective (a describing word for some other noun). It attaches itself to a noun to tell us something about what happened to it. For instance, in the phrase “*the **defeated** warrior*” the word “***defeated***” describes the “*warrior*”, telling us they’ve undergone defeat. In Sanskrit, you’d say जितः योधः where जितः means “defeated” and योधः means “warrior.” The PPP is like a snapshot of an action that’s already happened, frozen in time to describe something or someone.

Why should you care about the PPP? If you’re learning Sanskrit to read ancient texts, chant mantras, or explore Indian philosophy, the PPP is your best friend. It’s everywhere : in poetry, scriptures, and even modern Indian languages that borrow heavily from Sanskrit. For example, words like जित (meaning “conquered” or “defeated”) or दत्त (meaning “given”) in Hindi, Tamil, or Marathi often come from Sanskrit PPPs. If you’ve ever heard names like Devadatta (the one who is given by God) or Buddha (the awakened one), you’ve already encountered PPPs without realizing it! They’re not just grammar ; they’re part of the cultural fabric of India.

The PPP is also practical because it’s versatile. You can use it in two ways:

1. As part of a phrase: Like “*the defeated warrior*” जितः योधः, which describes something without forming a complete sentence.

2. As a full sentence: Like “*The warrior is defeated*” जितः योधः, where the same phrase can stand alone as a complete idea, implying the verb “is.”

In English, we distinguish these uses with word order or helper verbs like “is” or “was.” For example, “*the defeated warrior*” is a phrase, but “*The warrior is defeated*” is a sentence because of the verb “*is*.” Sanskrit, however, is more flexible. Its word order is free, and the verb “to be” (like “is” or “was”) is often optional. So, जितः योधः could mean either “the defeated warrior” (a phrase) or “The warrior is defeated” (a sentence), depending on context. This flexibility makes Sanskrit beautifully concise but can feel tricky at first. Don’t worry, we’ll explore this with examples to make it crystal clear.

Let’s now get to the nuts and bolts of how PPPs are created in Sanskrit. If you’re familiar with Indian languages like Hindi, Tamil, or Bengali, you’ll notice that many Sanskrit PPPs feel familiar because they’ve been borrowed into these languages. Hence, these are familiar although their formation is not fully systematic, which is good news for learners.

We saw gerunds like जित्वा that come from the verb root जि (meaning: “to conquer” or “defeat”) and means “after defeating.” To turn this into a PPP, you take the gerund and tweak its ending to match the gender and number of the noun it describes. Here’s the rule:

Replace the gerund’s ending वा by:

\- अः for masculine nouns, like रामः

\- आ for feminine nouns, like सीता

\- अम् for neuter nouns, like फलम्

So, from जित्वा, you get:

\- जितः for masculine, meaning “defeated”

\- जिता for feminine, meaning “defeated”

\- जितम् for neuter, meaning “defeated”

These forms act like adjectives, agreeing with the noun they describe in gender, number, and case. Let’s see this in action with some examples:

\- Masculine: “The defeated king” is जितः नृपः

\- Feminine: “The defeated illusion” is जिता माया

\- Neuter: “The defeated hell” is जितं नरकम्

Notice how the ending of जित changes to match the gender of the noun it describes like any normal adjective (e.g. धीरः, धीरा, धीरम्). This is a hallmark of Sanskrit adjectives, and it’s what makes the PPP so adaptable.

To make this concrete, let’s look at another verb root दृश् (meaning: “to see”). The gerund is दृष्ट्वा and hence the PPP forms are:

\- दृष्टः “seen” (masc)

\- दृष्टा “seen” (fem)

\- दृष्टम् “seen” (neut)

Now, let’s use these in phrases:

\- Masculine: “The seen sage” is दृष्टः मुनिः . This could describe a wise person who has been seen by others.

\- Feminine: “The seen goddess” is दृष्टा देवी. This could describe a goddess who is seen by a devotee.

\- Neuter: “The seen hell” is दृष्टं नरकम्. This might refer to an underworld realm glimpsed in meditation.

Now, let’s try another verb root कृ (meaning: “to do” or “make”). The gerund is कृत्वा, and the PPP forms are:

\- कृतः (masculine: “done” or “made”).

\- कृता (feminine: “done” or “made”).

\- कृतम् (neuter: “done” or “made”).

Examples:

\- Masculine: कृतः अपराधः “the offence done”

\- Feminine: कृता कृतिः “the done task”

\- Neuter: कृतं कर्म “the work done”

These examples show how the PPP can describe all kinds of nouns, from people to abstract concepts, in a way that feels natural and vivid.

The Two Uses: Phrase or Sentence?

One of the coolest things about the PPP is its flexibility. In English, we use PPPs in two ways:

1. As a phrase: “*The fallen leaf*” describes something without being a complete sentence.

2. As a sentence: “*The leaf is fallen*” is a full sentence, thanks to the verb “is.”

In Sanskrit, this distinction is less clear because of two unique features:

- Flexible word order: Unlike English, where word order is rigid and Sanskrit allows adjectives like PPPs to be placed anywhere relative to the noun. For example, जितः नृपः and नृपः जितः both can mean “*the defeated king*” or “*the king is defeated*.”

- Optional “to be” verb: In English, we need “is” or “was” to make a sentence like “The king is defeated.” In Sanskrit, the verb “to be” (like अस्ति) is often implied. So, जितः नृपः can be a phrase “*the defeated king*” or a sentence “*The king is defeated*”, depending on context.

This flexibility can feel confusing, but it’s also what makes Sanskrit concise and flexible. Context mostly helps you figure out the intended meaning.

The PPP is a favorite in Sanskrit because it’s a compact way to convey complex ideas. Instead of saying, “The king was defeated by someone and then he did something,” you can just say जितः नृपः and move on with the story regarding the something that he did afterwards. This efficiency is useful. Moreover, PPPs are familiar to speakers of Indian languages because they’ve been borrowed extensively.

Let’s explore a few more verbs to see the PPP in action:

\- Verb Root गम् → Gerund: गत्वा =\> PPP: गतः (masc) = gone

\- Verb Root भू → Gerund: भूत्वा =\> PPP: भूतः (masc) = been

The PPP isn’t just a grammatical trick ; it’s a window into Sanskrit’s literary and cultural world. In modern India, PPPs live on in names, prayers, and everyday language. By mastering PPPs, you’re not just learning grammar ; you’re unlocking the heart of Sanskrit’s expressive beauty.

The past passive participle is a cornerstone of Sanskrit, blending the action of verbs with the descriptive power of adjectives. Whether you’re reading about a “defeated king” जितः नृपः, a “seen goddess” दृष्टा देवी, or a “done deed” कृतं कर्म, the PPP helps you capture complex ideas with elegance and precision. Its flexibility - working as both a phrase and a sentence—makes it a favourite in Sanskrit literature, and its echoes in modern Indian languages make it relevant today. To summarise :

# Collecting Important Past Passive Participles

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In this final section, we collect and organize important examples of PPP. This first set of roots add इत्वा for the gerund and the इतः (masc) / इता (fem) / इतम् (neut) for the PPP. This is the default and regular way to form the past passive participle.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ypfQ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0f364c9f-8aa4-4b54-a567-3b07304c57ec_1492x1144.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ypfQ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0f364c9f-8aa4-4b54-a567-3b07304c57ec_1492x1144.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some roots just take the त् directly as a part of their gerund or PPP without any change. Most of them end in प्.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!t2SN!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd26f3e53-5b16-4d2e-8b6a-b717490c1a71_1484x630.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!t2SN!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd26f3e53-5b16-4d2e-8b6a-b717490c1a71_1484x630.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

What follows now are instances where the gerund and the PPP have त्वा and तः (masc) / ता (fem) / तम् (neut). All the verb roots in this category end in vowels.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!4wrl!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6e408c42-ad67-435e-89fa-8984e315d86c_1482x1036.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!4wrl!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6e408c42-ad67-435e-89fa-8984e315d86c_1482x1036.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kI6G!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F825ca35d-d3ec-40f3-8265-b9696a4d2e91_1482x874.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kI6G!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F825ca35d-d3ec-40f3-8265-b9696a4d2e91_1482x874.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Sometimes, roots ending in आ drop it and take an इ.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!luEz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff363ab0b-e532-41ad-b3cc-f9d366eef182_1466x192.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!luEz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff363ab0b-e532-41ad-b3cc-f9d366eef182_1466x192.heic)

Roots that end in ज् / च् when they take त् as a part of the gerund or the PPP make च्/ज् + त् = क्त्.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qVwN!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb15fe877-0b2f-48b2-8d7b-a5b7fc424d8d_1460x454.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qVwN!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb15fe877-0b2f-48b2-8d7b-a5b7fc424d8d_1460x454.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Such roots lose their nasal too mostly in the process while doing it.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vJRp!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0c87ce29-9a6c-4b27-b5fe-8adba612ed51_1476x374.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vJRp!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0c87ce29-9a6c-4b27-b5fe-8adba612ed51_1476x374.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Other roots that end in च्/ज् and many roots that end in श्/ष् convert them into an ष् and then assimilate the त् to an ट् in the gerund and the PPP.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!uvAO!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7da94b3a-abe4-4e35-a6fe-0b63a2f68bb3_1508x1292.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!uvAO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7da94b3a-abe4-4e35-a6fe-0b63a2f68bb3_1508x1292.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Now we present instances where the gerund and the PPP weaken initial व and य to उ and इ. The most popular among them are:

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tosm!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9bc83ca2-4cf4-4d59-8737-b6b5a3fb393e_1476x464.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tosm!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9bc83ca2-4cf4-4d59-8737-b6b5a3fb393e_1476x464.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For roots that end in a nasal, sometimes the nasal is lost in the gerund and the PPP.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!XJtc!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5c27b41a-6272-47b3-8f6d-d20897c56427_1474x538.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!XJtc!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5c27b41a-6272-47b3-8f6d-d20897c56427_1474x538.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Sometimes, the vowel अ in the root lengthens to आ to compensate for the loss of the nasal.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!q-C0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb591e8a5-3c64-466f-9fa7-69f385a5dcef_1474x220.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!q-C0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb591e8a5-3c64-466f-9fa7-69f385a5dcef_1474x220.heic)

For other roots that end in a nasal (especially when they end in रम्), the vowel still lengthens while the nasal is retained.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!J07I!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9d3302ab-6d7c-4dd6-9685-c84d1353df9f_1484x468.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!J07I!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9d3302ab-6d7c-4dd6-9685-c84d1353df9f_1484x468.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Roots that end in an aspirated voiced consonant and do not take an इ to form their gerund and PPPs assimilate the त् in the gerund and PPP suffix to the voiced and aspirated ध्.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!pUjK!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe5c4397b-c430-409b-9d62-63416a459607_1486x930.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!pUjK!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe5c4397b-c430-409b-9d62-63416a459607_1486x930.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Roots that end in ह् convert it into a ग् and then convert the त् in the gerund and PPP suffixes to ध्/ढ्.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8b1R!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F177b8351-4d39-44d8-a2c4-73ce0a9f763d_1508x1154.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8b1R!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F177b8351-4d39-44d8-a2c4-73ce0a9f763d_1508x1154.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some PPPs take the ending नः / ना / नम् instead of तः / ता / तम्. Many of them enter in the letter त् / द् / (ञ्)ज्.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xvn7!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fef88b522-597d-489b-b384-86fbcfa8cae0_1128x1178.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xvn7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fef88b522-597d-489b-b384-86fbcfa8cae0_1128x1178.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some PPPs are so irregular that they have to simply be memorised separately.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!si7M!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F971ab9d2-4e9c-4a95-99c3-ea7d836b5121_1108x286.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!si7M!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F971ab9d2-4e9c-4a95-99c3-ea7d836b5121_1108x286.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### Some Famous Names & Titles

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some famous PPP names for Indian figures, rituals, texts or rituals.

1.  अमितः - Amit is a very famous and popular name in the northern part
    of India. It comes from the Sanskrit PPP अमितः which is the opposite
    of मितः which is the PPP of the verb root मा that means measurement
    or bounding. The meaning of मितः literally means bounded or measured
    and hence अमितः means unbounded or infinite. Another famous Indian
    name Amitabh comes from the Sanskrit compound अमितभः whose final
    part भः comes from the verb root भा that means shining and hence
    अमितभः means infinite radiance. अमितभः also is the name of an
    important Bodhisattva in Mahāyāna Buddhism. In Mahayana Buddhism,
    there is a genre of sūtras called “The Perfection of Wisdom”
    (般若波羅蜜多) which includes the famous Diamond Sutra, Heart Sutra
    etc. Its Sanskrit name प्रज्ञापारमिता = प्रज्ञा (wisdom) + अपार
    (beyond) + मिता (scaled) which literally means a wisdom that is
    gotten by travelling beyond over to another side (transcending). She
    is also represented as a goddess (Devī) in Tibetan Buddhism.

2.  अवलोकितः - It comes from the prefix अव and the verb root लोक् which
    together means observing. In Buddhism, अवलोकितेश्वर is the name of a
    tenth level Bodhisattva associated with करुणा (compassion). Lord of
    all that is observed is the meaning of अवलोकितेश्वर.

3.  सुभाषितम् - The name given to a proverb in Sanskrit is सुभाषितम् which
    is again a PPP that literally means “spoken good” which translates
    to a good saying in English idiom. It comes from the verb root भाष्
    that means speaking.

4.  संस्कृतम् - The name of the Sanskrit language is again a PPP coming from
    सं(स्) \[well\] + कृतम् \[made\], referring to a refined language.

5.  वन्दिता & पूजिता - These two are famous names for Hindu girls that are
    also famous epithets of Hindu goddesses that are PPP meaning honored
    and worshipped, coming from the roots वन्द् and पूज् respectively.

6.  परिणीता - This word means a married woman in Sanskrit and is split
    etymologically as परि (around) + नीता (led) and hence literally
    means “led around” referring to the Hindu marriage ceremony where a
    woman is led around the Vedic fire by her husband during marriage.

7.  अनुगृहीतः - The sentence अनुगृहीतः अस्मि meaning “I am blessed” again
    contains a PPP that means blessed.

8.  अक्षितः - This word split as अ + क्षितः from the verb root क्षि that
    means exhaustion or decay and hence the name literally means
    inexhausted and extends to mean inexhaustible.

9.  भक्तः - This famous word that means “devoted” is the PPP of the verb
    root भज् that means to divide and separate out. The sense of devotion
    comes from the notion of separating out and setting something sacred
    to have it serve as an object of devotion.

10. सङ्गीतम् - This word referring to music comes from सं + गीतम् which the
    past participle from the root गै(गी) that means singing.

11. दलितः - This word invented in the modern times to refer to castes
    that are have suffered discrimination in India is actually a PPP
    that means broken and shattered.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Sanskrit’s Passive Obsession

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In English, we hardly use the passive voice unless we are being formal or we want to hide who is doing the action; we prefer to say “*Rama did the work*” rather than “*The work was done by Rama*”. But Classical Sanskrit, especially the later one goes in time, prefers using passive participle in the past tense to express who did what. This tendency becomes so high eventually that it eclipses the usage of any proper past tense active or passive verb (remember that Sanskrit has three past tenses and this past passive participle manages to eclipse in frequency, all these past tense forms combined!!). For e.g., the famous Sanskrit story collection ***Hitopadeśa*** hardly has any past tense verbs and only has the past passive participle instead. So, in Sanskrit, be prepared to expect more frequently, the passive forms like रामेण कृतः कार्यः (The work \[was\] done by Rama), rather than the active रामः कार्यम् अकरोत् (Rama did the work). This is true especially in dramas. Some practice examples are given below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!aJa6!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe51760e2-0a37-4a11-8310-06aca81bdafd_1658x956.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!aJa6!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe51760e2-0a37-4a11-8310-06aca81bdafd_1658x956.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!eu1u!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Feb51ac37-b664-43f9-aa24-93c8b016fa09_2048x1145.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!eu1u!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Feb51ac37-b664-43f9-aa24-93c8b016fa09_2048x1145.png "Screenshot 2025-10-31 at 21.02.43.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

This preference for passivity in past tense using past participles that are technically adjectives is so dominating in later Sanskrit that it almost eclipses the three past tense verbs of Sanskrit. For example, this is seen in dramatic texts. Consider a random dialogue from Abhijñānaśākuntalam where Śakuntalā says “*O Anasūya, a young needle of Kuśa grass has pierced my foot*”. Note how this comes about in the actual Sanskrit :

> अनसूये, अभिनव-कुश-सूच्या परिक्षतं मे चरणम्

Literal translation: O Anasūya, by a young needle of Kuśa grass, my foot \[has been\] pierced

Instead of the expected active voice form with a main active verb

> अभि-नव-कुश-सूची चरणं मे **पर्यक्षणोत्  **
> *A young needle of Kuśa grass \[has\] **pierced** my foot*

Let us first read some short passages to get acquainted with PPPs.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5IX5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F48ccaafb-56f9-4e2e-8d90-1e6550e8f692_2048x1138.png)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# The Enigmatic Locative & Genitive Absolute

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In this section, we will learn about a pair of syntactical constructions that are logically easy to follow but kind of hard to get used to because they have no counterpart in English or any modern Indian language as well. They are called by Sanskrit grammarians as सति सप्तमी & सति षष्ठी and by Western grammarians as **locative absolute** & **genitive absolute**. If you have studied ancient Greek or Latin by any chance, what I am going to teach now is the exact counterpart of the genitive absolute in ancient Greek and the ablative absolute in Latin.

Before that let us recollect the location form and the “of” form of the nouns that we have seen so far for masculine-neuter and feminine nouns.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1-MD!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6d710aae-31e3-4cf2-bfe7-706bd37ab027_1132x238.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1-MD!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6d710aae-31e3-4cf2-bfe7-706bd37ab027_1132x238.heic)

Let me illustrate the locative absolute with an example right away.

> **भोजने खादिते** बालः निद्रां करोति

This sentence literally translates to:

> *In-food in-eaten, the child sleeps.*

This does not make sense when literally translated into English. But to translate it into slightly more comprehensible English while staying true to the Sanskrit idiom, it would be:

In \[the event of\] food \[having been\] eaten, the child sleeps.

The actual message that the sentence is trying to convey in normal English is either one of the following:

> **भोजने खादिते** बालः निद्रां करोति  
> *After the food was eaten, the child sleeps.  
> Because the food was eaten, the child sleeps.  
> After eating the food, the child sleeps.  
> Because he ate the food, the child sleeps*

This is actually synonymous to the following sentence that you already saw:

> **भोजनं खादित्वा** बालः निद्रां करोति

So, in Sanskrit, if you want to say

> *After X-ing Y,* ……  
> *Because Y was X-ed*, …..  
> *After Y was X-ed*, …

then, you take the PPP of X, the noun Y and put both of them in the location form (in/on/at).

If instead of the location (in/on/at) form, we have the “of” form in the above construction, then the sentence conveys that the main event happened in spite of the PPP event happening before it. This construction is called the genitive absolute. An example is:

> **बहुषु सैनिकेषु हतेषु** राजः युद्धे अजयत्  
> *After/Because many soliders were killed, the king won in the battle*
>   
> (7th case - locative absolute)
>
> **बहूनाम् सैनिकानाम् हतानाम्** राजः युद्धे अजयत्  
> ***In spite of many soldiers having been killed**, the king won the
> battle  *
> (6th case - genitive absolute)

This is not that difficult to understand but still an unusual construction and can be difficult to literally translate. Here are many examples to familiarise yourself.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!sSi0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd02eaa42-90ff-4fbe-9c2c-2a12b0e5b98d_2048x1116.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!sSi0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd02eaa42-90ff-4fbe-9c2c-2a12b0e5b98d_2048x1116.png "Screenshot 2025-10-31 at 21.16.48.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In the first stanza of the composition Bhaja Govindam by Śaṅkarācārya, one sees:

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!fR-P!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F988e6255-bf33-4793-8216-e30846e39cc7_2048x1158.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!fR-P!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F988e6255-bf33-4793-8216-e30846e39cc7_2048x1158.png "Screenshot 2025-10-31 at 21.20.16.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!smSO!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F672096ab-9540-46cd-bdff-4df6c102b85d_1344x542.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!smSO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F672096ab-9540-46cd-bdff-4df6c102b85d_1344x542.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Note** : In Latin, this construction exists and is called the *ablative absolute* because the past participle and the verb takes the ablative case (fifth case - “from” form). Some popular examples

1.  Law - the popular phrase still used in legal discourse “*mutatis
    mutandis*” is one such construction that means “after the things to
    be changed are changed”. A locative absolute version in Sanskrit
    that means the same would be “परिवर्तितव्येषु परिवृत्तेषु” .

&nbsp;

2.  Ancient Romans before their Christian conversion, used to keep dates
    from the time the city of Rome was founded. In modern Christian
    dating system, this dates to 753 BCE. So, all events were dated
    since that time. The year would be quoted as “*ab urbe condita*”
    which literally is an absolutive construction that means “after the
    city was founded”. So, 750 BCE in that system would be three years
    after the city’s founding and hence be reported as “3 anni ab urbe
    condita” = 3 years after the city was founded. The parallel locative
    absolutive construction in Sanskrit would be “नगरे प्रतिष्ठिते”

&nbsp;

3.  Another example from Newton’s Principia

    Latin original : “*Tum ordinatis in infinitum imminutis, adeo ut
    coincidant curvae cum axe AB*”

    Translation : “Then, after the coordinates are reduced to infinity,
    so that the curves coincide with the axis AB”

    The phrase “*ordinatis imminutis*” is like sati saptamī and means
    “after the coordinates are reduced”. A Sanskrit parallel would be
    “यामेषु नष्टेषु”

Ancient Greek and Old Church Slavonic, also use this absolutive construction. Here are some more examples for practice.

1.  **रामेण वनं गते दशरथः अश्रूणि सृजति।**  
    After the forest had been gone to by Rāma, Daśaratha sheds tears

2.  **सूर्ये उदिते बालकाः क्रीडन्ति।**  
    After the sun had risen, the boys play.

3.  **आचार्ये आसीने छात्राः तिष्ठन्ति।**  
    After the teacher had been seated, the students stand.

4.  **वर्षायां आरब्धायां कृषकाः हृष्यन्ति।**  
    After the rain has begun, the farmers rejoice.

5.  **द्वारे पिहिते कोऽपि न प्रविशति।**  
    After the door has been closed, no one enters.

&nbsp;

6.  **भोजने कृते बालकः क्रीडितुम् अगच्छत्।**  
    After the meal had been made, the boy went to play.

7.  **पुस्तके लिखिते सः विश्रामं करोति।**  
    After the book had been written, he rests.

8.  **कार्ये समाप्ते ते गृहम् अगच्छन्।**  
    After the work had been finished, they went home.

9.  **सन्देशे प्राप्ते सः आनन्दितः अभवत्।**  
    After the message had been received, he became happy.

10. **राजेन आज्ञायां दत्तायां मौनम् आसन्।**  
    When the order had been given by the king, all were silent.

&nbsp;

11. **ग्रामे दग्धे जनाः अन्यत्र अगच्छन्।**  
    After the village had been burned, the people went elsewhere.

12. **शत्रौ जिते सैनिकाः आनन्दन्।**  
    After the enemy had been defeated, the soldiers rejoiced.

13. **आचार्येण दत्ते उपदेशे छात्राः प्रबुद्धाः अभवन्।**  
    After the instruction had been given by the teacher, the students
    became enlightened.

14. **नद्यां तीर्णायां वयम् विश्रामं अकुर्वन्।**  
    After the river had been crossed, we took rest.

15. **पत्रे लिखिते सः तत् अप्रेषयत्।**  
    After the letter had been written, he sent it.

&nbsp;

16. **रामेण वने गते अयोध्यायां शोकः अभवत्।  **
    After Rāma had gone to the forest, there was sorrow in Ayodhya.

17. **युद्धे आरब्धे सर्वे सैनिकाः सज्जाः अभवन्।**  
    After the battle had begun, all the soldiers became ready.

18. **धने नष्टे सः चिन्तायाम् अपतत्।**  
    After the wealth had been lost, he fell into worry.

19. **आचार्ये उपविष्टे छात्राः ध्यानम् अकुर्वन्।**  
    After the teacher had sat down, the students meditated.

20. **कार्येषु समाप्तेषु अधिकारी निर्णयम् अकरोत्।**  
    After the tasks had been completed, the officer made a decision.

Now, let us see examples of **genitive absolutes** (especially in later and less strictly Pāṇinian usage), though they’re much rarer than the standard **locative absolute**

1.  **रामेण वनस्य गतस्य अपि न शोचति।**  
    Even though Rama has gone to the forest, he does not grieve.

2.  **आचार्येण वचनस्य उक्तस्य अपि शिष्यः न शृणोति।**  
    Even though the word has been spoken by the teacher, the student
    does not listen.

3.  **वर्षस्य पतितस्य अपि कृषिकाः न हृष्टाः।**  
    Even though rain has fallen, the farmers are not excited.

4.  **कार्यस्य समाप्तस्य अपि सः न विश्राम्यति।**  
    Even though the work has been finished, he does not rest.

5.  **राजस्य आगतस्य अपि ते न तुष्टाः।**  
    Even though the king has arrived, they are not pleased.

6.  **सत्यस्य उक्तस्य अपि जनाः न विश्वसन्ति।**  
    Even though the truth has been spoken, people do not believe.

7.  **अन्नस्य भुक्तस्य अपि बालः रोदिति।**  
    Even though food has been eaten, the child cries.

8.  **द्वारस्य उद्घाटितस्य अपि कोऽपि न प्रविशति।**  
    Even though the door has been opened, no one enters.

9.  **शास्त्रस्य पठितस्य अपि सः न जानाति।**  
    Even though the scripture has been studied \[by him\], he does not
    know.

10. **सूर्यस्य उदितस्य अपि तमः न निवर्तते।**  
    Even though the sun has risen, the darkness does not disappear.

11. **धनस्य प्राप्तस्य अपि ते असन्तुष्टाः।**  
    Even though wealth has been obtained, they are dissatisfied.

12. **पुस्तकस्य लिखितस्य अपि छात्राः न पठन्ति।**  
    Even though a book has been written, students do not read.

13. **शत्रोः जितस्य अपि राजा न गर्वं करोति।**  
    Even though the enemy has been defeated, the king is not proud.

14. **देहस्य क्लान्तस्य अपि सः कर्म करोति।**  
    Even though the body is tired, he works.

15. **गृहस्य निर्मितस्य अपि ते न तत्र वसन्ति।**  
    Even though the house has been built, they do not live there.

16. **दोषस्य दृष्टस्य अपि सः न वदति।**  
    Even though the fault has been seen, he does not speak.

17. **मार्गस्य ज्ञातस्य अपि ते भ्रमन्ति।**  
    Even though the path has been known, they wander.

18. **विद्यायाः अधीतायाः अपि सः मूर्खः एव।**  
    Even though learning has been acquired, he remains foolish.

19. **समयस्य अतीतस्य अपि कार्यं न कृतम्।**  
    Even though the time has passed, the work has not been done.

20. **वस्तुषु चोरितेषु अपि ते अदुःखिताः सन्ति  **
    Even though \[their\] things have been stolen, they do not become
    sad.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# The Story of Purandaradāsa

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In this section, we will read an extended passage and revise all that we have learnt so far. It is about a Kannada saint named **Purandaradāsa** who is fondly referred to as the *Saṅgītapitāmaha* as he played a foundational role in the Carnatic music tradition of South India and developed a series of songs that serve as graded lessons for learners of Carnatic music even to this day.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PjdQ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9fd980c3-6ba7-4edc-9801-033d8cb55f98_2048x1147.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Verbs Related to Creativity and Production

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In this vocabulary building section, we will build verbs that are related to composing and creating things. The normal and generic verb that is used to convey making anything is करोति that also means doing.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kAKI!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F16851355-a7e1-457a-a803-e6bebde51f4f_1116x334.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kAKI!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F16851355-a7e1-457a-a803-e6bebde51f4f_1116x334.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The verb that is used for explicit creative action to convey that the subject actively created or developed or caused or arranged or set in motion something is रचयति.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vQGk!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa577f0e5-846f-4d96-bf72-6d72bfe3e957_1118x336.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vQGk!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa577f0e5-846f-4d96-bf72-6d72bfe3e957_1118x336.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Another verb that means creation but more in the sense of emission, production, drawing out or release is सृजति from which we get the noun सृष्टिः.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1GEQ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fee4500f9-89a9-4b72-bbc3-7c48aaa58711_1126x328.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1GEQ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fee4500f9-89a9-4b72-bbc3-7c48aaa58711_1126x328.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Another verb that means to design, build and establish in an analytical context is निर्माति.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Fp5X!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd6e1aa45-5a8e-4a77-a830-773383c148de_1124x340.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Fp5X!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd6e1aa45-5a8e-4a77-a830-773383c148de_1124x340.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The verb that is used to denote compiling and arranging and creating a large intricately compiled and connected narrative or structure is ग्रथ्नाति.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!RGdl!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F56b9a260-50fe-4da0-9723-6782e3c74f03_1132x324.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!RGdl!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F56b9a260-50fe-4da0-9723-6782e3c74f03_1132x324.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The verb that is used to denote generation or causing the birth or emergence of someone or something or making someone become is जनयति.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vAaz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2cf75553-3cb9-4417-9db8-73360ef955eb_1114x320.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vAaz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2cf75553-3cb9-4417-9db8-73360ef955eb_1114x320.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The verb that is used to denote creation in the sense of arising is उत्पादयति.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!UXLI!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2cbb6b95-ebb9-47ae-8beb-b63c3b065ba6_1122x324.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!UXLI!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2cbb6b95-ebb9-47ae-8beb-b63c3b065ba6_1122x324.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The verb that is used to denote dispensing or providing is विदधाति and is related to the famous noun विधाता that means provider and it occurs in the Indian national anthem too. It is an irregular verb and behaves like the verb ददाति that means giving.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CLxw!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7377380b-afd0-42c9-ae1d-97be95265af7_1126x336.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CLxw!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7377380b-afd0-42c9-ae1d-97be95265af7_1126x336.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

One another final verb that occurs less frequently but is used to denote literally the delivery of a child and is metaphorically extended to giving life and impelling something to existence is सौति/सुवति whose participle is सुतः. It is a commonly used adjective to introduce anyone as the son of someone - for e.g, वीरसेनसुतः as the son of वीररसेनः.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rJh-!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fdab76cba-83ca-4608-8b6d-deb936713542_1130x340.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rJh-!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fdab76cba-83ca-4608-8b6d-deb936713542_1130x340.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Try forming your own simple sentences using all the verbs that you have learnt so far.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
